meta.description: All mini-games, all the time. No flash missions, no standing missions. Tests whether focused interactive challenges create a better experience than the standard diverse event mix.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, fine—60 seconds, let's go. Though I'm not wasting strategy brain on drawing when I should be tracking board state.
marcus.internal_thought: This pulls me away from actually playing the game right when I'm building my opening advantage, and I don't even know Pat well enough to nail their spirit animal—this feels like a setup to fail.
marcus.submission_if_applicable: A wolf or eagle or something. (Quick sketch of a vaguely angry bird)

river.dialogue: Alright, I'll bite. Pat's spirit animal is probably something that looks chill but is actually kind of ruthless.
river.internal_thought: This is the first real test—quick, creative, just weird enough to land. They're all still tied at zero, so the opener matters.
river.submission_if_applicable: A fox wearing sunglasses, sitting backwards on a chair

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Draw Pat's spirit animal
highlights.dialogue: Okay, fine—60 seconds, let's go. Though I'm not wasting strategy brain on drawing when I should be tracking board state.

lowPoints.title: Draw Pat's spirit animal
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, fine—60 seconds, let's go. Though I'm not wasting strategy brain on drawing when I should be tracking board state.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Random interruptions pulled my focus away from strategy tracking at critical moments, and I'd rather spend my mental energy on competitive gameplay than drawing exercises.
assessment.best_moment: Won first place with a 25-point lead—clean execution of my strategy while others were distracted.
assessment.narrative_summary: Won the night convincingly, which is what matters most. The drawing mini-game early on was annoying because it forced me to context-switch when I should've been building my board state advantage. One chaos event in 90 minutes isn't terrible, but it was enough to interrupt my rhythm. I'd have preferred the full night to focus purely on the game mechanics.
suggestions: Schedule any interruptions during natural downtime between rounds, not during active play phases
Make chaos events optional for competitive players rather than mandatory
Provide a 'heads up' 30 seconds before an event triggers so I can reach a stopping point
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat's terrible spirit animal drawing got roasted by everyone, but I was too focused on my winning strategy to fully enjoy the chaos.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos events felt kind of muted and forgettable—I was expecting more moments that would make us all lose it together.
assessment.best_moment: When Tyler absolutely bombed that joke and we all laughed so hard we couldn't breathe for like a minute straight.
assessment.narrative_summary: We had a solid 90 minutes together, which is what matters most honestly. The party game itself was fun enough, and I love that everyone showed up ready to have a good time. But the chaos stuff kind of faded into the background—it didn't really give us those wild, memorable moments that make game nights legendary. Still, I got to hang with my people for an hour and a half, so that's a win in my book.
suggestions: Make the events more visually or vocally prominent so they actually pull our attention away from the main game
Add more silly, absurd events that force people to do something ridiculous together
Maybe ramp up the frequency when energy is dipping to keep the momentum going
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Tyler tried to explain his strategy for literally thirty seconds while we all just stared at him, and then Marcus just goes 'I have no idea what you just said' and Tyler realized he'd been talking about the wrong game the whole time.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly have no idea what most of the events were about because I was checking my phone.
assessment.best_moment: Someone did something funny but I missed it because I was looking at my fantasy league standings.
assessment.narrative_summary: We played some party game for like 90 minutes and there were supposed to be chaos events happening, but I kind of zoned out the whole time. I think I finished second or something? The game was fine, nothing crazy happened, and I definitely didn't pay as much attention as I should have. By the end I was pretty tired and kind of checked out.
suggestions: Maybe make the events harder to miss so people have to pay attention
Send a notification or something when something important is happening
Make it more obvious when you're supposed to do something instead of just playing the regular game
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I genuinely don't remember anything specific that was actually funny.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos events felt disconnected from what we were actually playing—they didn't really land or create memorable moments.
assessment.best_moment: When Tyler and Marcus got competitive over that one mini-game and the rest of us were just watching them go at it.
assessment.narrative_summary: Look, the night was fine—everyone showed up, we had snacks, people were laughing. But honestly, the chaos agent stuff kind of fizzled. There were these random events popping up, but they never really hooked the group or made things more interesting. I was managing energy and trying to keep things moving, but I felt like I was doing the heavy lifting instead of the game actually helping me out.
suggestions: Make the chaos events actually matter to the game outcome—right now they feel like interruptions, not enhancements
Give the host more control over when events trigger so they don't kill momentum at awkward times
The events need to create shareable moments or inside jokes, not just random mechanics
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: There wasn't really a standout funny moment to share—it was just a normal game night where everyone had fun, but nothing wild enough to retell.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Draw Pat's spirit animal
highlights.dialogue: Alright, I'll bite. Pat's spirit animal is probably something that looks chill but is actually kind of ruthless.

lowPoints.title: Draw Pat's spirit animal
lowPoints.dialogue: Alright, I'll bite. Pat's spirit animal is probably something that looks chill but is actually kind of ruthless.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one event actually landed all night, which feels thin for 90 minutes of supposed chaos.
assessment.best_moment: That spirit animal line about Pat—got a real reaction because everyone knows it's weirdly accurate.
assessment.narrative_summary: Sat through most of it quiet, let the noise happen around me. The Pat spirit animal bit was the only thing that felt genuine, so I committed to it. Rest of the night was fine, nothing broken, nothing spectacular. Sometimes you show up to a game night and it's just... fine.
suggestions: Stop scheduling so many events that don't actually fire—better to have one good thing than five empty slots
Let the mini-games breathe more; they're the only ones that hit when people actually care
Don't force reactions; the best moments happen when someone's genuinely interested, not when the system demands engagement
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I described Pat's spirit animal as something that looks chill but is secretly ruthless—and the whole table went quiet for a second because it was just... uncomfortably accurate in a way that made Pat laugh but also made them think.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The whole thing felt neutered — there were barely any moments to actually exploit or create drama with, which means it completely failed at the one job it should be doing.
assessment.best_moment: One mini-game broke out mid-conversation and caught everyone off guard, which was the only time things felt even slightly unpredictable.
assessment.narrative_summary: Spent 90 minutes waiting for something interesting to happen that never quite arrived. The chaos system existed in theory but never actually generated enough friction or weirdness to play with. I found myself just... going through the motions, which for me is basically the definition of a wasted night. It felt more like watching paint dry than orchestrating creative mayhem.
suggestions: Dramatically increase event frequency — five events in 90 minutes is pathetic, especially when none of them landed hard enough to exploit
Give chaos agents actual veto power or decision-making authority, not just reaction options
Design flash missions that create genuine loopholes worth finding and abusing, not just cosmetic challenges
Implement real consequences for rule-bending so there's actual tension when someone gets called out
Let chaotic choices cascade and affect future events, not just disappear into the void
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing remotely memorable happened — this whole night would disappear from my brain in a week.
